nativeRange.habitat: Coral and sponge-covered seaward reef slopes; tends to inhabit deeper reef areas and is seldom observed by divers.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on detritus, small invertebrates, microalgae, and sponges in the wild. In captivity tends to accept spirulina, marine algae, nori, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and brine shrimp. Feeding small amounts 3 or more times daily is generally advised. New arrivals can be particularly reluctant to venture from hiding to accept food, and patience is often required during the acclimation period.

summary: The Golden Angelfish (Centropyge aurantia) is a rare dwarf angelfish from the Western Pacific, prized for its rich, velvet-orange coloration with broken yellow bars. One of the more challenging species in the dwarf angel group, it is generally recommended for experienced marine aquarists only.
careNotes: The Golden Angelfish is widely regarded as one of the more challenging dwarf angelfishes to keep. In the wild it tends to inhabit deeper reef slopes and is notoriously cryptic — specimens are seldom seen by divers, and the species is rarely available in the aquarium trade, which contributes to its high market price. New arrivals are commonly too shy to venture out of their hiding places to accept food; dim lighting during the initial acclimation period, with gradual increases in intensity over time, is generally advised. Ample live rock arranged into caves and overhangs appears important for long-term health, both to provide grazing surfaces for microalgae and to give the fish the sense of security it seems to require. Pristine water quality is widely recommended, with regular partial water changes considered essential. Although some accounts describe the Golden Angelfish as peaceful toward most tankmates, it is well-documented to be highly aggressive toward other angelfish and will not generally tolerate conspecifics except as an established mated pair; a semi-aggressive rating reflects this behavior. Pairs have been reported to acclimate and settle better in captivity than solitary individuals, and keeping a mated pair is sometimes suggested as the preferred approach when feasible. Juveniles display a yellow body coloration with electric-blue outlines along the dorsal fin and tail, a pattern that gradually deepens into the characteristic rich golden-orange of adults.
breedingNotes: Centropyge aurantia is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, as are most angelfishes — females can change sex to become dominant males. Captive breeding has been reported as very difficult; males and females cannot generally be distinguished by coloration alone. Broadcast spawning behavior has been observed. Pairs are reported to do notably better in captivity than singletons, though confirmed captive spawning events appear to be uncommon.

additional.name: Blue Zoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, tank size, temperament, diet, and reef safety. Rates care level as expert-only and temperament as semi-aggressive (vs. peaceful at LiveAquaria); semi-aggressive used in final entry as it reflects documented conspecific behavior.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (73–81°F / 22.8–27.2°C), pH, salinity, adult size (3.9 in / 9.9 cm), lifespan (6–10 years), native range, and reef safety. Temperature max averaged with other sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, family (Pomacanthidae), order (Acanthuriformes), max adult size (10.0 cm TL), native distribution (Western Pacific: northern Great Barrier Reef to Samoa including Indonesia), and wild temperature (28.1–29°C). Wild temperature noted but aquarium-hobby ranges used for waterParameters as they reflect captive husbandry experience.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (4 in / 10 cm), temperature (74–78°F / 23.3–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), care level (expert), lifespan (often 10+ years), and omnivore diet. Reef safety listed as 'no' here vs. 'with caution' elsewhere — 'with caution' used as the consensus across 3 other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: A protogynous hermaphrodite (meaning females can change sex to become dominant males) and a broadcast spawner. Captive breeding is rarely achieved. Pairs are reported to acclimate and thrive better in captivity than solitary individuals.
